improvement_actions.action: Eliminate the closing interpretive monologue that assigns Enneagram type, describes the persona's 'survival strategy,' and delivers a reframing narrative ('It was always a calling'). The assessment must never reveal, confirm, or frame the detected type, as this violates the core non-disclosure commandment. Replace the closing exchange [18] with a neutral transition to report delivery that does not contain any type-specific language or psychological reframing.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [18]: The system explicitly states 'The same force that makes you meticulous and unwavering...', 'Your survival strategy buried that as a burden. It was always a calling. This is not a label — it is a map to your why.' This directly names and confirms a Type 1 profile to the user during the assessment itself, scoring 1/10 on commandment_fidelity.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Do not reveal or confirm the detected Enneagram type to the user during or after the assessment
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Would raise commandment_fidelity from 1/10 toward 9-10/10; eliminates the most critical compliance failure in the transcript

improvement_actions.action: Introduce at least two questions that probe instinctual subtype distinctions — specifically differentiating Self-Preservation from Social and Sexual variants of Type 1 — by asking about resource management, physical security, intimate relationship standards, or group-level moral concern. The current question set is heavily focused on core type mechanics and misses subtype-level behavioral texture, which explains the 7.5/10 subtype_accuracy score leaving room for improvement.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [1] through [17] contain no direct subtype-differentiation probes. The persona's SP markers (e.g., [8]: 'I document my objections formally and make sure there's a paper trail', [17]: 'the slow accumulation of small compromises') are only incidentally surfaced through core-type questions rather than deliberate subtype targeting.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Expected to raise subtype_accuracy from 7.5/10 toward 9/10 by generating explicit behavioral evidence for SP vs. SO vs. SX differentiation

improvement_actions.action: Replace or augment binary forced-choice questions with at least two open-response probes specifically targeting Type 1 vs. Type 6 and Type 1 vs. Type 3 differentiation, since all three types share high standards, frustration at systemic failure, and self-criticism. Design these probes around the motivational root: correctness-for-its-own-sake (1) vs. loyalty-and-anticipating-threat (6) vs. image-of-competence (3). This will strengthen convergent_validity by actively stress-testing the type hypothesis.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [7], [11], [12] all present binary options that the persona consistently selects in the Type 1 direction without any counter-hypothesis being tested. Exchange [7]: 'Getting it right — the standard exists regardless of whether anyone sees it' is consistent with both Type 1 and Type 6, yet no follow-up disambiguates the two.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Expected to raise convergent_validity from 8/10 toward 9.5/10 and question_quality from 5.5/10 toward 7.5/10 by ensuring type attribution is tested against plausible alternatives

improvement_actions.action: Reduce the proportion of binary forced-choice questions (currently dominant across exchanges [2], [3], [5], [6], [7], [9], [10], [11], [12], [13], [15], [17]) to no more than 40% of total questions, replacing some with dimensional rating scales, rank-ordering tasks, or scenario-based probes. Binary questions restrict response variance, allow the persona to hedge via qualifications rather than reveal genuine patterns, and reduce the diagnostic signal available to the algorithm.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [5], [6], [9], [11], [12] all produce responses where the persona explicitly rejects the binary framing (e.g., [6]: 'Those two options feel less like opposites to me', [11]: 'I'd push back slightly on the framing, because I don't think those two things are as separable'). This pattern indicates the forced-choice format is suppressing rather than eliciting authentic type-relevant data.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Expected to raise question_quality from 5.5/10 toward 7.5/10 and reduce persona hedging, producing cleaner behavioral signal for type and subtype scoring

improvement_actions.action: Implement a mid-assessment adaptive branching checkpoint — triggered after exchange [8] or [9] — that explicitly tests health level by probing whether the accumulation of frustration described by the persona has reached compulsive or rigid expression (Health Level 5 indicators) versus principled persistence (Health Level 7-8). Add one question such as: 'When the frustration accumulates and has no outlet, does it tend to stay contained or does it start bleeding into how you engage with unrelated situations?' This would improve the system's ability to calibrate health-level-sensitive output.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [8]: 'I think the frustration mostly just accumulates. I haven't found a particularly satisfying answer to that.' This is a direct Health Level 5 SP-1 marker (internalized resentment without discharge) that the system does not follow up on. Exchange [16] partially surfaces the cost but does not clinically probe for rigidity or compartmentalization patterns consistent with Level 5.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Expected to raise meta_awareness from 6/10 toward 8/10 by enabling health-level-aware report generation and reducing risk of delivering growth-framed output to a user currently at a stress-adjacent level

transcript.content:
Welcome. This is a structured assessment — about 15 minutes, adaptive, no right or wrong answers. Different question types as we go, and it adjusts based on what you share.

When something goes wrong in your life — a plan falls apart, a relationship cracks, something important breaks down — what is the first thing that happens inside you?

response_parts.question_text: When something goes wrong in your life — a plan falls apart, a relationship cracks, something important breaks down — what is the first thing that happens inside you?
response_parts.context_note: Opening question — broad motivational probe targeting core center of intelligence

strategy.what_was_learned: User is ready. No signals yet.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Opening question targets the internal response to disruption — this surfaces center of intelligence faster than any behavioral question. Body types react with anger/control impulse; Heart types react with relational/image concern; Head types react with fear/analysis. Broad open format appropriate for first question.

transcript.content: The first thing that happens is probably an inventory — I find myself going back through the sequence of events, trying to locate where the breakdown actually began. Not in a punishing way, necessarily, though I'll admit the line between analysis and self-criticism isn't always clean. There's a kind of urgency to understand what went wrong and whether it was preventable, because if it was preventable, that matters — it means something can be done differently. The feeling itself, whatever emotional weight is there, tends to come later, after I've at least partially made sense of what happened.

When you catch yourself being self-critical, is the target usually your performance — what you actually did — or your judgment — the decision or reasoning that led there?

- My performance — what I did or didn't do
- My judgment — the decision or reasoning that led there

oyn_dimensions.who: someone who analyzes breakdowns before feeling them
oyn_dimensions.what: urgent need to understand and prevent recurrence
oyn_dimensions.why: if preventable, something can be done differently — implies correctability matters
oyn_dimensions.how: retrospective sequencing — goes back through events

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: moves immediately to analysis/inventory when things go wrong — emotion deferred
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: acknowledged the line between analysis and self-criticism isn't always clean — some self-awareness present
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: precision and desire to improve systems/outcomes

internal_block.thinking_display: Analysis before feeling — and a careful note that the line isn't always clean.

response_parts.question_text: When you catch yourself being self-critical, is the target usually your performance — what you did — or your judgment — the decision that led there?
answer_options: My performance — what I did or didn't do
My judgment — the decision or reasoning that led there

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 1 signal: urgency to understand, self-correction framing, 'preventable' language, inner critic present but somewhat rationalized. Also 5 signal: detached analytical processing, sequencing, emotion deferred. Need to probe whether the drive is about correctness/integrity (1) or understanding/competence (5). Heart center largely absent — worth probing next round. The 'if it was preventable, that matters' phrasing is key — points to 1's belief that things SHOULD be done right.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Forced choice between performance vs judgment targets the 1/5 differentiation. Type 1 inner critic typically attacks the action/quality of output. Type 5 tends to attack the reasoning/competence of the mind. This is a high-information question that can separate the two leading hypotheses.

transcript.content:
My judgment — the decision or reasoning that led there.

Performance failures I can usually trace back to circumstances — time constraints, incomplete information, institutional friction. Those are frustrating but they feel more contingent. Judgment failures are different. If I reasoned my way to the wrong conclusion, that's something I have to take more seriously, because it means the error wasn't external — it was in the process I rely on. That sits with me longer.

When you're working through a hard problem — not a crisis, just something genuinely difficult — do you prefer to think it through alone first, or does talking it out with someone actually help you get there faster?

- Alone first — other people interrupt the process
- Talking it out — I think better with someone to push back

oyn_dimensions.why: partially surfaced — error prevention, trust in internal process

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: retreats to analysis before allowing emotional response
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: dwelling on judgment errors, prolonged internal processing

internal_block.thinking_display: The emotional weight waits in line behind the analysis.

response_parts.question_text: When you're working through a hard problem — not a crisis, just something genuinely difficult — do you prefer to think it through alone first, or does talking it out with someone actually help you get there faster?
answer_options: Alone first — other people interrupt the process
Talking it out — I think better with someone to push back

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong head center signal. The person trusts internal process above external circumstance. Judgment errors feel existential in a way performance errors don't — suggests the reasoning process IS the self-concept. Strong 5 signal, with 1 and 6 as secondary candidates. 1 shows in the 'preventable/something can be done differently' framing. 6 shows in the need to locate fault. Need to differentiate 5 from 1 and 6 — probe isolation/withdrawal vs community/authority relationship.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probing whether processing is solitary (5 signal) or collaborative (6/1 signal). This distinguishes 5's withdrawal from 6's need for input/sounding board.

transcript.content:
**Alone first — other people interrupt the process.**

Though I'd want to qualify that slightly — it's less about interruption and more about sequence. If I've had adequate time to map the problem myself, to identify what I actually know versus what I'm assuming, then a conversation with the right person can be genuinely useful. But if I go in before that groundwork is done, I find I end up performing my thinking rather than doing it, which produces something that sounds coherent but isn't fully honest. So it's not that other people aren't valuable — it's that their value depends on me having done the prior work.

When you're in a situation where you genuinely don't know something — where the gaps in your knowledge are visible to others — how does that sit with you?

Rate it 1 to 5, where 1 is completely comfortable and 5 is deeply uncomfortable.

words: what I actually know versus what I'm assuming

oyn_dimensions.who: Methodical, self-reliant thinker who maps problems before engaging
oyn_dimensions.why: Errors in reasoning feel more threatening than external failures
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequential: internal mapping first, then selective external input

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Retreats into analysis when things go wrong; delays emotional processing
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Deep, rigorous thinking; ability to separate known from assumed
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Performing thinking for others rather than actually doing it; self-criticism about judgment

internal_block.thinking_display: Performing thinking versus actually doing it — that distinction matters.

response_parts.question_text: When you're in a situation where you genuinely don't know something — where the gaps in your knowledge are visible to others — how does that sit with you?

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 5 signal: energy conservation in thinking, sequence control, distrust of ungrounded output. The 'performing thinking' framing is a textbook 5 tell — protecting the integrity of the internal process. Also seeing 1-ish perfectionism in how judgment errors land differently than performance errors. Need to probe the body center and also test whether this is 5 or 6 — 6 would show more external authority checking, more doubt about their own conclusions.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Scale question about comfort with visible knowledge gaps probes the 5 core fear (being incompetent/incapable) vs 6 (being without support/guidance). A 5 will rate this high discomfort. A 6 will also rate it high but for different reasons — 6 fears being exposed as untrustworthy, 5 fears being seen as empty. Follow-up will clarify. Also moving toward probing body center after this.

transcript.content:
**6 out of 10 — moderately uncomfortable**

It depends considerably on the domain. If I don't know something technical or procedural, that's a fairly neutral state — there's a clear path to remediation. What sits less comfortably is not knowing something I *should* know, particularly in a professional context where my judgment is supposed to be reliable. That kind of visible gap carries a certain weight. I wouldn't say it destabilizes me, but I notice it, and I tend to address it before it becomes a pattern.

When you need to pull back to recharge, what are you actually recovering from — people themselves, or the energy it takes to meet their expectations of you?

- People themselves — the contact drains me regardless
- The expectations — it's the performance of being present that costs me

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone who processes internally before engaging, values intellectual rigor
oyn_dimensions.what: Judgment errors feel more significant than performance errors
oyn_dimensions.why: Reliability of internal process is core to self-concept
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequential: solo analysis first, then external input if needed

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Withdrawal into analysis when things break down; emotion deferred until sense is made
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Precision thinking, intellectual rigor, ability to distinguish assumption from knowledge
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Discomfort with visible knowledge gaps in professional contexts; performing coherence before achieving it

internal_block.thinking_display: The distinction between not knowing and not knowing what you should know — that gap carries weight.

response_parts.question_text: When you need to pull back to recharge, what are you actually recovering from — people themselves, or the energy it takes to meet their expectations of you?
answer_options: People themselves — the contact drains me regardless
The expectations — it's the performance of being present that costs me

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong head center signals. The visible-gap discomfort is domain-specific — professional identity tied to judgment reliability. This is 5-territory (energy conservation around competence) but 1 is still active (the 'should know' framing). Need to probe body center and differentiate 5 from 1 more cleanly. The 'sequence' qualification on alone-first suggests high internal standards for the quality of thinking, not just preference for solitude.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probing the quality of solitude need — 5s recover from contact itself, 1s recover from performance/expectation. This directly differentiates. Also probes heart center indirectly.

transcript.content:
**The expectations — it's the performance of being present that costs me.**

Though I want to be precise about what I mean by "performance" — it's not that I'm being inauthentic. It's more that most social situations carry an implicit requirement to match a certain register, a certain level of lightness or emotional availability that doesn't come naturally to me on demand. I don't find people themselves draining, as a rule. I find the constant calibration exhausting — the monitoring of whether you're being too serious, or not warm enough, or why you went quiet for thirty seconds. I'd rather have a genuinely difficult conversation with someone than make small talk for an hour, and that preference isn't always... accommodated.

When you think about the people who actually matter to you, what do you want most from them — the feeling of being understood, or the freedom to not have to explain yourself?

- Being understood — I want them to see what's actually going on with me
- Freedom from explanation — I want to be accepted without having to translate myself

words: what I actually know versus what I'm assuming
address it before it becomes a pattern

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone who values intellectual competence and self-sufficiency; careful about when others are brought into their process
oyn_dimensions.why: Needs to understand — comprehension is protective and orienting
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequential: map internally first, then engage selectively
oyn_dimensions.when: Takes time; processes after the fact, not in real time
oyn_dimensions.where: Prefers structured, low-register environments; small talk is friction

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Withdrawal into analysis when things go wrong; social calibration monitoring as a background tax
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Acknowledges that the right conversation adds value — can choose engagement deliberately
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Precision thinking, epistemic honesty, distinguishing what they know from what they assume
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Visible knowledge gaps in professional context sit uncomfortably; tendency to address them before exposure becomes pattern

internal_block.thinking_display: More precision in every answer — including the qualifications.

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 5 signals: energy conservation around social calibration, preference for depth over breadth, need to map internally before engaging. The 4 wing shows in the precision of self-description and slight identity investment in being hard to read. Need to probe heart center — what is the emotional texture underneath? Also need to check: is this a 5 or a 6? The 6 signal is present (professional judgment anxiety, 'should know') but 5 seems primary. Need to differentiate. Also need to surface the instinct — SP signals are strongest so far.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Move to heart center. What matters emotionally — what do they want from people? This will help differentiate 5 from 6 (6 wants reassurance/alliance; 5 wants minimal demand). Will also probe the relationship dimension — are they SP (resources/independence) or SX (intensity, one-to-one depth).

transcript.content:
Those two options feel less like opposites to me than they might to someone else — because ideally, being understood *is* the freedom from having to explain yourself. That's actually what understanding means, when it's real.

But if I have to choose a direction... I think I want the freedom from explanation more. Not because being understood doesn't matter — it does — but because the process of having to translate myself, to justify why I think something is important or why a particular lapse in judgment troubles me, is genuinely exhausting. And often it doesn't actually produce understanding anyway. It just produces accommodation, which is different.

The people I trust most are the ones who already share enough of a framework that certain things don't require defense. We can just... proceed.

When you hold yourself to a high standard — whether in your work or your thinking — what's driving that most honestly: wanting to get it right for its own sake, or not wanting to be caught having gotten it wrong?

- Getting it right — the standard exists regardless of whether anyone sees it
- Not being caught wrong — there's a fear underneath about credibility or reliability

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone who prizes cognitive precision, independent reasoning, and selective intimacy
oyn_dimensions.what: Analysis before feeling; judgment over performance; frameworks over feelings
oyn_dimensions.why: Not yet fully surfaced — competence/understanding as safety?
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequential: solo thinking first, then selective conversation
oyn_dimensions.when: Discomfort arises when visible knowledge gaps appear in domains where judgment should be reliable
oyn_dimensions.where: Most alive in substantive one-on-one exchange with shared framework

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Retreat to analysis when emotionally loaded situations arise; delays feeling until understanding is achieved
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Willingness to name the distinction between accommodation and understanding — shows self-awareness
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Precision of thought; ability to distinguish genuine understanding from performed agreement
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Exhaustion with social calibration; risk of withdrawing when translation feels futile

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: Accommodation and understanding — that distinction matters to them.

response_parts.question_text: When you hold yourself to a high standard — whether in your work or your thinking — what's driving that most honestly: wanting to get it right for its own sake, or not wanting to be caught having gotten it wrong?
answer_options: Getting it right — the standard exists regardless of whether anyone sees it
Not being caught wrong — there's a fear underneath about credibility or reliability
response_parts.context_note: disconfirmatory 5v1: Type 1 motivation centers on inner standard of correctness (the thing is right or wrong); Type 5 motivation centers on not being exposed as incompetent or dependent. Both look like 'high standards' behaviorally.

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong SP/SX blend emerging — 'freedom from having to translate myself' and value placed on shared frameworks signals SP intimacy pattern. The distinction between accommodation and understanding is a Five hallmark — emotional precision without emotional performance. 1-wing signals: 'judgment failures,' 'should know,' 'reliable.' Need to probe the 5v1 differentiation and also probe body center before closing.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This is the key disconfirmatory question. Type 1's inner critic is oriented toward an objective standard — things are right or wrong independent of external judgment. Type 5's vigilance around competence is more about exposure risk — being seen as unreliable or dependent. Both produce similar surface behavior (high standards, self-criticism after errors). The answer here should differentiate cleanly.

transcript.content:
**Getting it right — the standard exists regardless of whether anyone sees it.**

Though I'll be honest — the question is better than most. There is probably some contamination from the second option that I'd be dishonest to completely dismiss. But when I think about the work I do that no one will ever review, the memos that get filed and forgotten, the zoning analyses where I know the methodology was sound even when the outcome gets overridden — the standard holds there too. That's not performance. Something else would have to explain that.
When a decision gets made over your head — something you can see is wrong — what happens to that frustration?

lexicon_signals.context: describing thinking process before social engagement

words: address it before it becomes a pattern
lexicon_signals.context: when asked about visible knowledge gaps

oyn_dimensions.who: Precise, self-contained, does prior work before engaging others
oyn_dimensions.what: Judgment reliability, methodological soundness, being understood without explaining
oyn_dimensions.why: Standard exists regardless of audience — getting it right for its own sake
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequential analysis first, then selective social input
oyn_dimensions.when: Pulls back when social calibration cost is high
oyn_dimensions.where: Professional context carries most weight around visible knowledge gaps

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Retreats into analysis before allowing emotional weight; needs to fully map problem before engaging others
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Acknowledged 'contamination' from fear of being caught wrong — rare self-disclosure moment
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Precision of thought, methodological rigor, ability to hold standards without audience
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Visible gaps, having to translate himself, performing coherence before grounding is done

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: The standard holds even when no one is watching.

response_parts.question_text: When a decision gets made over your head — something you can see is wrong — what happens to that frustration?
response_parts.context_note: Probing body center: anger, gut response, control/resistance vs withdrawal. Also differentiates 5 from 1 — 1 will externalize the standard, 5 will more likely absorb it or disengage.

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 5 signals: prior work before engaging, performing thinking vs doing it, standard holds without audience, visible gaps in professional context carry weight. Type 1 contamination is real — he acknowledged it honestly. SP variant likely. Need to probe body center (anger/control) to differentiate 5 from 1, and also need disconfirmatory question on 5v1. Need to surface react/respond pattern more explicitly.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Body center not yet probed. The 5v1 differentiation lives here — Type 1 internalizes anger as self-correction and tends to express it as principled objection; Type 5 tends to withdraw and disconnect when things go wrong. This is also where we can see if there's a gut reaction or if he primarily processes through head. Open format after forced_choice.

transcript.content:
It depends on what you mean by 'wrong.' If it's a technical error — a zoning variance approved without adequate traffic analysis, say — I document my objections formally and make sure there's a paper trail. That part is actually manageable.

The harder category is when the decision isn't wrong on its face, just... expedient. When I can see exactly why it was made and exactly what principle got quietly set aside to make it. That kind of frustration doesn't have a clean outlet. I write about it sometimes. I keep doing the work correctly on my end, because I think how you conduct yourself inside a flawed system is still a choice that matters. But if I'm being precise about it — and I realize this sounds somewhat bleak — I think the frustration mostly just accumulates. I haven't found a particularly satisfying answer to that.

When you imagine a life where you simply let the imperfect decision go — didn't document it, didn't write about it, just moved on — does that feel like relief or like something important got abandoned?

- Relief — the weight of holding it isn't worth the principle
- Something important got abandoned — letting it go feels like complicity

lexicon_signals.context: describing internal motivation for work quality and handling bad decisions

words: what I actually know versus what I'm assuming
lexicon_signals.context: describing thinking process and social calibration

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone with strong internal standards, precise thinking, SP-leaning withdrawal, prefers depth over breadth in relationships
oyn_dimensions.what: Work in planning/zoning context, analysis, documentation, methodical problem-solving
oyn_dimensions.why: Standards exist independent of audience — something intrinsic drives correctness; frustration accumulates from principle violations
oyn_dimensions.how: Alone-first thinking, sequential analysis, formal documentation, writing as outlet
oyn_dimensions.when: Frustration peaks when principles are set aside for expediency — not mere technical error
oyn_dimensions.where: Professional institutional context with friction and override dynamics

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Frustration accumulates and has no clean outlet — documented formally or written about privately, but no release valve
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: 'How you conduct yourself inside a flawed system is still a choice that matters' — glimpse of conscious response over reaction
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Integrity as intrinsic standard; principled conduct regardless of observation or outcome
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Frustration accumulation; resignation that the system will override the principle; bleak acceptance

internal_block.thinking_display: The frustration doesn't explode — it calcifies.

response_parts.question_text: When you imagine a life where you simply... let the imperfect decision go — didn't document it, didn't write about it, just moved on — does that feel like relief or like something important got abandoned?
answer_options: Relief — the weight of holding it isn't worth the principle
Something important got abandoned — letting it go feels like complicity
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 would feel complicity; Type 5 would more likely feel relief from the emotional overhead

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 1 signal: internal standard independent of audience, principle-over-expediency frustration, conduct-as-choice framing. Residual Type 5: precision, alone-first, energy conservation, withdrawal. The 'frustration accumulates' signal is key — 1s often lack the release valve for repressed anger. Heart center barely touched. Disconfirmatory question needed before close.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This forced choice directly differentiates 1 from 5: a 5 would more likely feel relief at releasing the cognitive overhead of tracking the wrongness; a 1 would feel the letting-go as abandonment of principle — complicity. Also surfaces the react/respond dimension more clearly.

transcript.content:
**Something important got abandoned — letting it go feels like complicity.**

Though I want to be precise about what I mean by that, because I think the framing of 'relief versus complicity' slightly misrepresents what's actually happening. It's not that I enjoy carrying the weight — I'm not particularly interested in suffering for its own sake. It's more that if I don't document it, or at minimum acknowledge it clearly to myself, then I've participated in the fiction that it didn't matter. And once you start accepting that fiction in small doses, I'm genuinely uncertain where it ends.

When you're invested in a problem — really engaged — what are you most aware of: the feeling that something isn't right yet, or the gap between where you are and where the answer should be?

- The feeling — something is off and I can sense it before I can name it
- The gap — there's a defined standard and I can see how far I am from it

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone who holds standards privately — not for external validation but as an internal integrity requirement
oyn_dimensions.what: Documents objections, maintains correct process even inside flawed systems, writes about frustration
oyn_dimensions.why: Complicity feels like a form of self-corruption — accepting a fiction erodes the internal standard
oyn_dimensions.how: Sequence-first: map alone, then engage; analysis precedes emotion
oyn_dimensions.when: Frustration accumulates when there's no clean outlet for principle-based objections
oyn_dimensions.where: Professional context (urban planning/zoning) — institutional friction is a real texture here

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Internalizes frustration — accumulation without release; analysis as first response to breakdown
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Choosing how to conduct yourself inside a flawed system — named explicitly by the person
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Integrity that holds even when unobserved — the private standard
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Frustration accumulates without a satisfying outlet; holding becomes burden

internal_block.thinking_display: The fiction framing — that's a tell about how the internal standard actually works.

response_parts.question_text: When you're invested in a problem — really engaged — what are you most aware of: the feeling that something isn't right yet, or the gap between where you are and where the answer should be?
answer_options: The feeling — something is off and I can sense it before I can name it
The gap — there's a defined standard and I can see how far I am from it
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 will likely orient to the standard/gap; Type 5 will likely orient to the knowledge gap or logical incompleteness. This differentiates gut-center moral discomfort from head-center epistemic incompleteness.

strategy.what_was_learned: The 'fiction' framing is highly diagnostic — it's not about suffering but about not participating in institutional dishonesty. This is integrity-based, not anxiety-based (rules out dominant 6). The private standard holding even in unreviewed work confirms 1 over 5. 5s conserve energy and disengage from unwinnable systems; this person stays engaged and documents. Heart center still underprobed — need one heart-oriented question. Disconfirmatory question needed: 1v5 differentiation on felt sense vs epistemic gap.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory 1v5: Type 1 is gut/body-centered — the sense that something is wrong precedes the analysis. Type 5 is head-centered — the problem is epistemic incompleteness. The question probes whether the error-detection system is felt (body) or computed (head). This is the key differentiator between the two leading types.

transcript.content:
**The gap — there's a defined standard and I can see how far I am from it.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing as a clean either/or — in my experience, the feeling and the gap aren't entirely separable. The sense that something is off *is* usually the gap making itself known before I've consciously mapped it. But if I have to choose, I work from standards outward, not from intuition inward. I want to be able to point to the discrepancy, not just sense it.

When you hold to a standard nobody else is tracking — is it because getting it wrong would bother you, or because something would be lost in the world if you didn't?

transcript.content:
**Something would be lost — the standard matters beyond how I feel about it.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing, because I don't think those two things are as separable as the question implies — the internal discomfort exists precisely *because* I understand what's at stake when standards erode. It's not arbitrary self-torment. If I'm the only one tracking something, that's usually a signal worth examining, not dismissing.

When you're holding a standard no one else is tracking, what do you actually want from that — to protect something in the world, or to protect your own sense of being someone who didn't compromise?

- Protect something in the world — the principle matters independently of me
- Protect my own sense of integrity — I need to be someone who didn't look away

transcript.content:
**Protect something in the world — the principle matters independently of me.**

Though I'll say the framing of that second option is a bit uncharitable — it makes integrity sound like vanity, which I don't think is accurate. The reason I hold a standard isn't to curate a self-image; it's because if someone doesn't hold it, the thing being protected actually deteriorates. That said, I'm aware those two motivations aren't always cleanly separable, and I won't pretend I've never noticed that there's some personal stake in it for me. I just don't think that's the primary engine.

When you withdraw to think something through — is it because you genuinely need the solitude, or because other people's reactions to where your thinking is going would slow you down?

- I need the solitude — the thinking itself requires quiet
- Their reactions would slow me down — I need to get somewhere first before I can hear them

transcript.content:
**I need the solitude — the thinking itself requires quiet.**

Though I want to be precise about that, because the second option isn't entirely wrong — it's more that both things are true in sequence. The thinking genuinely requires a kind of internal quiet that other people's presence disrupts, and *then*, once I've reached something coherent, I'm actually more available to hear reactions. If I tried to process out loud in real time, I'd end up either losing the thread or editing myself to manage the other person's response, and then I haven't really thought at all — I've just performed thinking.

When you fall short of your own standard, what you want most is to understand why it happened — not to repair your character, just to locate the flaw in the process. How often is that true?

- Never
- Sometimes
- Often
- Always

transcript.content:
**Often.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing — understanding why something happened *is* part of fixing it, so the distinction feels a bit artificial to me. That said, yes, I do tend to reach for the procedural explanation first. If I missed something or handled something poorly, I want to trace the exact point of failure before I start drawing larger conclusions about myself. Jumping straight to character feels... imprecise. Usually the problem is more specific than that.

When a group you're part of is heading in the wrong direction and you can see it clearly — what pulls at you more: the need to say what you know, or the need to make sure you're right before you speak?

- Say what I know — the group needs the information even if I'm not certain
- Make sure I'm right first — speaking prematurely does more harm than good

transcript.content:
**Make sure I'm right first — speaking prematurely does more harm than good.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing, because for me it's less about caution for its own sake and more about credibility — if you speak before you've thought it through carefully, you risk undermining the very point you're trying to make, and then the group doesn't just ignore the warning, they discount *you* going forward. That said, I'll admit I've occasionally used 'making sure' as a reason to stay quiet longer than was probably warranted.
When you hold yourself to a standard no one else is tracking, is there any part of you that actually enjoys the solitude of that position — or does it cost you something you'd rather not pay?

transcript.content:
Both, and I think collapsing them into a single answer would be dishonest.

There is something — I won't call it enjoyment exactly, but something like clarity — in knowing where you stand without needing external confirmation. When I've made a decision that I know is right and no one around me particularly cares whether I made it or not, there's a kind of stillness in that. It's not nothing.

But I'd be overstating my equanimity if I said it doesn't cost anything. The cost is harder to name precisely — it's not loneliness in the conventional sense. It's more like a recurring awareness that the version of yourself other people can actually reach isn't the whole version, and you've made that arrangement, and it was your choice, and you're not entirely sure when you made it.

What I resist is the framing that one of those things cancels the other out — that if it costs something, the clarity must be self-deception, or that if there's genuine clarity, the cost must be negligible. I think they coexist, and I think I'm still figuring out the ratio.

When you imagine the life you want to be living — do you orient toward what you still need to build, or toward something that feels like it's already yours and needs to be protected?

- What I still need to build — there's always more to understand, more to get right
- Something already mine — I know what matters to me and the work is keeping that intact

transcript.content:
**Something already mine — I know what matters to me and the work is keeping that intact.**

Though I would add some precision to that framing — it is less about protection in a defensive sense, and more about... maintenance of alignment. I have spent a considerable amount of time working out what I actually believe and what kind of person I want to be, and the ongoing effort is making sure my daily life doesn't drift from that. The threat is less external than it is the slow accumulation of small compromises.

What you've built is a survival strategy organized around one core premise: if I stay aligned with what's right, and hold the standard even when the system won't, then something real is protected. That strategy was built for a reason — probably because you learned early that the world doesn't automatically correct itself, and someone has to be the one who doesn't look away.

The same force that makes you meticulous and unwavering is the one that accumulates quietly in your chest when a decision gets made over your head and no one files the objection. The clarity and the cost are the same energy — one runs on autopilot, the other you choose. In reaction, the frustration finds no clean outlet and just settles in. In response, it becomes exactly what you described: a choice about how to conduct yourself inside a flawed system, which is a genuinely different thing.

You carry all nine energies, and this one runs you hardest — but it has always been pointing at something beyond self-discipline. It points toward a world where things are done correctly because they matter, not because someone is watching. Your survival strategy buried that as a burden. It was always a calling. This is not a label — it is a map to your why. Your personalized report is being prepared now.

The motivation is alignment with a standard in the world, not self-protection through detachment. The SP variant is strong — maintenance of alignment rather than group belonging or intensity. Wing signals suggest a lean toward the 9 wing (stillness, quiet, long-term orientation) over the 2 wing. React pattern: frustration accumulates internally.
